metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'alert' concretely in AC 4 — e.g., state the default as 'emit a `log.error` with a structured key recoverable via grep AND increment the manifest's `recovery_failures` tally by 1' so both the test assertion and the operational behavior are unambiguous.
findings.finding: 'Raises an alert' in AC 4 is undefined — an executor cannot determine what observable artifact constitutes an alert (log.error line, Prometheus counter increment, PagerDuty event, manifest field, etc.) without stopping to clarify.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 4: 'a recovery failure raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally' with: 'emits a `log.error("recovery_failure", ...)` structured log entry AND increments `manifest["recovery_failures"]` by at least 1; the test asserts both the log output and the manifest field value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC #6 (or add AC #7) to also require `python -m pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q` green, covering both in-scope test directories declared in Scope.
findings.finding: AC #6 only validates `tests/scripts/bridge/` green, but the Scope section explicitly lists `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` as an in-scope test location. The unit tests added there have no passing criterion, creating an uncovered deliverable and a cross-section contradiction between Scope and the acceptance gate.
findings.location: AC #6 (`python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ -q`) vs. Scope section (`tests/scripts/bridge/` AND `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/`)
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC #6 to: '`python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q` green', or add a separate AC #7: '`python -m pytest tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q` green'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC#6's proving command to include `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` (e.g., `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q`) so all five functional ACs have a mandatory green gate.
findings.finding: AC#6 specifies `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ -q` as the sole proving command, but the Scope section explicitly names `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` as the test landing zone for the new tests. All five functional ACs (retry, dedup-guard, bind_pending write-ahead, recover_pending_bindings client fix, lease steal) map to unit-test additions in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` — none of these are covered by `tests/scripts/bridge/`.
findings.location: AC#6: '`python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ -q` green'; Scope section: 'plus tests under `tests/scripts/bridge/` and `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC#6 with: '`python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q` green (covers the new retry, dedup-guard, bind_pending, recovery-client, and steal unit tests alongside existing bridge smoke tests)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether Jira Cloud REST v3 includes a `Retry-After` header on 429 responses (vs. `X-RateLimit-*` headers), and update AC1 and What §1 to mirror ADR 0036's 'honor Retry-After iff present, else jittered backoff' fallback contract — so the behavior is well-defined and testable regardless of Jira's actual header set.
findings.finding: AC1 asserts 'Retry-After honored' for the REST-path 429 retry without verifying that Jira Cloud REST v3 actually emits a Retry-After header on 429 responses, nor specifying a fallback when the header is absent. ADR 0036 explicitly flagged the equivalent question as unverified for the subprocess path and adopted a defensive hedge ('honor Retry-After iff present'); no equivalent hedge appears for the REST path. urllib.error.HTTPError.headers is technically accessible, but if Jira REST returns X-RateLimit-* headers instead of Retry-After (the pattern documented in Atlassian's rate-limit guidance), the 'honored' behavior becomes vacuous and tests that inject the header artificially will green regardless.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; What §1 ('Revive the retry layer')
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the ADR 0036 language in AC1: change 'simulated 429 with Retry-After honored' to 'simulated 429 with Retry-After honored when present; jittered backoff when absent'. In What §1, add the same hedge. This is already implemented for the subprocess path (_rate_limit_backoff in acli_subprocess.py) and just needs to be carried forward.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define in AC4 the exact mechanism for 'alert' (e.g., write a `BRIDGE_ALERT` event file with tag `binding-recovery-failed`) and 'nonzero manifest tally' (e.g., increment `mutation_failures` in the reconcile result dict / manifest JSON) so the AC is unambiguously testable.
findings.finding: AC4 references 'raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally' as the observable for recovery failures, but never defines what concrete mechanism constitutes 'an alert' (a BRIDGE_ALERT event file, a stderr log line, an exception?) or 'nonzero manifest tally' (incrementing mutation_failures in reconcile.py's persist phase, writing to a manifest JSON, or something else). The codebase has three distinct 'alert' surfaces (BRIDGE_ALERT files at dispatch_one.py:283-285, stderr at run_differs.py:354, and exception propagation) — an implementer cannot determine which satisfies the criterion without a definition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §4: 'a recovery failure raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally instead of a silent swallow'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally' with: 'writes a BRIDGE_ALERT event file tagged `binding-recovery-failed` into the affected ticket's tracker dir, and the reconcile pass result's `mutation_failures` count is incremented by 1 for each failed recovery; a test asserts both observables.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in What §3 / AC3 how recovery handles the crash-between-create-and-label case where no rebar-id label exists yet: either (a) store the provisional jira_key in the pending record before calling add_label so recovery can confirm by key lookup rather than label JQL, or (b) acknowledge that such issues are unbound and re-created, and adjust AC3's 'no duplicate issue' guarantee accordingly.
findings.finding: The write-ahead recovery protocol in What §3 and AC3 claims that a crash between create_issue and add_label 'leaves a pending record that the next pass recovers via a working search_issues client.' However, the actual recovery in binding_store.recover_pending_bindings (line 450) uses label-based JQL (`labels = "rebar-id:{local_id}"`). An issue that crashed before add_label was called has NO rebar-id label — making it invisible to the JQL search. The existing code unbinds such issues (line 459). The plan does not specify this fallback: does the pending binding get unbound (lost), retried on the next pass, or trigger a separate alert?
findings.location: What §3 ('bind_pending + persist the binding store before create_issue; confirm after label attach') and AC3 ('a crash injected between create and label leaves a pending record that the next pass recovers')
findings.suggested_fix: In bind_pending (or a new bind_pending_with_key(local_id, jira_key)), store the provisional jira_key in the pending record immediately after create_issue returns it, before add_label is called. Recovery can then check `entry['jira_key']` first and confirm or verify that key exists, bypassing label-based JQL for the crash-between-create-and-label window.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the AC 6 proving command to cover both test directories: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q`.
findings.finding: The proving command `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ -q` omits `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/`, which the Scope section explicitly names as a test target. ACs 3 (bind_pending/crash recovery via binding_store.py), 4 (recover_pending_bindings client fix), and 5 (lease steal via _ref_lock.py) have tests that almost certainly live in `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/` — not `tests/scripts/bridge/` — so the stated proving command would pass even if those tests are absent or broken.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 6 / Scope section (tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC 6 command with: `python -m pytest tests/scripts/bridge/ tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/ -q`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC asserting that a non-retryable 4xx HTTPError (e.g. 404) raised inside a callable wrapped by `_call_with_retry` propagates to the caller as the original raw `urllib.error.HTTPError` without transformation or swallowing.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that a non-retryable 4xx HTTPError (e.g. 404, 403) propagates raw and unmodified THROUGH `_call_with_retry` to its caller — only that acli_rest.py's raw raise is unchanged. The 8+ callers that catch raw HTTPError in their own try/except blocks depend on this passthrough, but the new retry wrapper could silently swallow or re-wrap non-retryable errors.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1; What section item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'A test also asserts that a non-retryable HTTPError (e.g. 404) passed through _call_with_retry reaches the caller as the original raw HTTPError (verifying the passthrough contract for the 8+ existing catchers).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC asserting that a 429 injected AFTER an add_comment that already landed causes the retry to detect the existing comment-marker and short-circuit without posting a duplicate comment.
findings.finding: The plan's What section item 2 explicitly states that comment dedup (comment-marker check) is a non-idempotent write requiring a dedup-aware callable, but no acceptance criterion includes a test for retry-after-comment that short-circuits on an existing comment marker — only create/issue dedup is tested in AC2.
findings.location: What section item 2; Acceptance Criteria AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: '… similarly, a 429 after an add_comment that already landed asserts NO duplicate comment is created (comment-marker dedup check on retry).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_call_with_backoff` assertion: grep confirms it does not exist in `acli_subprocess.py` (ADR 0036 explicitly called it 'dead code left untouched'); update the Why finding #4 and the Scope OUT clause to reflect the actual current state of the subprocess retry machinery.
findings.finding: `_call_with_backoff` is asserted to exist in `acli_subprocess.py:236-292` and is described as having 'zero production call sites', but the function does not exist in the current codebase at all. ADR 0036 (docs/adr/0036-acli-429-rate-limit-backoff.md) confirms it was already dead code 'left untouched' as optional follow-up cleanup — meaning the bulk-path 429 fix (C4) already resolved the subprocess retry story. The plan's 'Why' diagnosis (finding #4) lists this as an inert retry layer to revive, but there is nothing to revive. Work AC items or scope text referencing `_call_with_backoff` as a live artifact would target a ghost function.
findings.location: Why section, finding #4 ('_call_with_backoff (acli_subprocess.py:236-292) has zero production call sites')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'acli_subprocess.py:236-292's `_call_with_backoff` has zero production call sites' with a note that `_call_with_backoff` no longer exists in the codebase (removed/never landed); the subprocess retry story is fully handled by `_run_acli`'s built-in loop (C4/ADR 0036). Remove the 'Out: `_call_with_backoff` revival (C4)' clause as it has no referent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify or remove the `_call_with_backoff (acli_subprocess.py:236-292)` reference in the Why section — the function does not exist at that path/range; note that the inline backoff logic now lives inside `_run_acli` (acli_subprocess.py:310+) and confirm whether the C4 boundary claim still holds.
findings.finding: `_call_with_backoff` at `acli_subprocess.py:236-292` does not exist. The function name is hallucinated — `acli_subprocess.py` has no function named `_call_with_backoff`; lines 236–292 contain `_decode_partial` and `_reap_process_group`. The plan's Why section (Finding #4) describes this as 'dead code with zero production call sites', but a function that doesn't exist cannot be an edit target or a scoping rationale.
findings.location: Why section, Finding #4: '`_call_with_backoff` (`acli_subprocess.py:236-292`) has zero production call sites'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '_call_with_backoff (acli_subprocess.py:236-292) has zero production call sites' with a description of `_run_acli`'s inline retry loop (acli_subprocess.py:310+) and note it is out-of-scope for the same reason (C4/subprocess path). Update the Scope Out section accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in What §4 whether `steal()` (full observe-wait-CAS, accepting the lease-length stall) or `try_break_if_stale()` (CAS-only, for callers that already observed and waited) is intended, and add operator-facing guidance (e.g. log the wait duration or emit a progress message during the sleep).
findings.finding: The plan directs calling `steal()` for expired leases but does not acknowledge that `steal()` at `_ref_lock.py:534` blocks synchronously for `first.lease_secs` (default 120 s) before attempting the CAS — leaving the implementer without guidance on whether to accept a 2-minute startup stall or use the lower-level `try_break_if_stale()` (which requires the caller to have already waited).
findings.location: What §4 / AC5: 'A pass encountering an expired lease steals it via steal() and proceeds'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise What §3 to specify that `create_issue`'s returned `jira_key` must be written back into the pending record (e.g. `bind_pending_with_key(local_id, jira_key)` or a separate `update_pending_key()` call) and the store persisted BEFORE `add_label`, so `recover_pending_bindings` can confirm by `jira_key` lookup (not only by label search) when the label is absent.
findings.finding: The write-ahead recovery mechanism cannot close the targeted orphan-create window because `recover_pending_bindings` is label-search-based: `bind_pending` writes `jira_key: None` before `create_issue`; after a crash between `create_issue` and `add_label`, the orphan issue exists in Jira but carries no `rebar-id:` label, so the recovery's `search_issues('labels = "rebar-id:{local_id}"')` returns empty, `unbind()` is called, and the next pass re-creates — the same duplicate the plan claims to prevent.
findings.location: What §3 / AC3: 'bind_pending is written and the store persisted before create_issue; a crash injected between create and label leaves a pending record that the next pass recovers via a working search_issues client (no duplicate issue)'
findings.suggested_fix: After `create_issue` returns `jira_key`, call a new `bind_pending_update_key(local_id, jira_key)` + `save()` before `add_label`. Then in `recover_pending_bindings`, if a pending entry has a non-None `jira_key`, call `bind_confirm` directly without a label search (the key is known); only fall through to label search when `jira_key` is None (create itself was interrupted). This closes the targeted window without changing the recovery interface.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path for the lease-steal change — e.g., a config flag (`REBAR_LEASE_STEAL_ENABLED`, default true) that reverts to exit-3 behavior, or document a named operator procedure (e.g., 'set env var X to disable steal') so the change is reversible without a code deployment.
findings.finding: The lease-steal behavioral change (expired lock → steal and proceed instead of always exit 3) is described as intentional but the plan provides no rollback/back-out path if `steal()` misfires in production (e.g., expiry check races, clock skew between hosts, or the steal mechanism has a latent bug that causes a split-brain pass).
findings.location: What #4 (`__main__.py` held-lock path); AC #5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retirement/quarantine path for pending binding records that fail recovery: specify whether they are retried indefinitely, capped at N attempts, or moved to a quarantine store with a distinct alert, so the RETIRE leg of the binding lifecycle is complete.
findings.finding: The plan makes recovery failures LOUD but does not define what happens to the pending binding record after a failed recovery attempt — whether it remains pending and is retried on the next pass, is quarantined, or accumulates indefinitely. This leaves the RETIRE/cleanup path of pending records unspecified for the failure branch.
findings.location: What §3 / AC: 'recover_pending_bindings receives a client object exposing search_issues; a recovery failure raises an alert and a nonzero manifest tally instead of a silent swallow'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `recovery_attempts` counter field to the pending record (or a side-file). After M failed recovery attempts, move the record to a `quarantine/` namespace in the store, emit a distinct CRITICAL alert with the rebar-id, and exclude it from subsequent automatic recovery so it does not re-fire every pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for steal() failure in __main__.py: distinguish a CAS-loss (another instance won — treat as exit 3 / live holder) from a transient I/O error (log + exit 3 with a distinct message) so monitoring can differentiate 'lock contention' from 'infra failure'.
findings.finding: The plan adds a steal() call in __main__.py for expired leases but specifies no error handling if steal() itself fails (e.g., git network error, concurrent steal CAS collision). The failure mode — whether the process exits 3, crashes with an unhandled exception, or proceeds incorrectly — is undefined.
findings.location: What §4 / __main__.py held-lock path (lines 534-539)
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the steal() call in a try/except; catch a 'lost CAS race' exception (if _ref_lock raises a distinct type) and exit 3 with message 'lease stolen by peer'; catch other exceptions, log the traceback, and exit with a distinct code (e.g., exit 4 'lease infrastructure error') so the AC 'live unexpired lease still exits 3' remains unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename the plan title to reflect the actual implementation (e.g., 'Wire reconciler safety: bind_pending write-ahead, lease steal(), and HTTPError-aware retry') so the title matches the body's explicit rejection of boundary translation.
findings.finding: The plan's title says 'retry-layer error translation' but the body explicitly rejects translation (HTTPError→JiraAPIError) and instead extends _call_with_retry to recognize HTTPError directly. The title implies a different implementation than what the body specifies, which could cause a reviewer or implementer to misread the intent.
findings.location: Title vs. What section item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to: 'Wire the reconciler's dead safety machinery: bind_pending write-ahead, lease steal(), and HTTPError-aware retry (no boundary translation)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing (a) the dedup-aware callable pattern as the required wrapper for all non-idempotent Jira writes and (b) the bind_pending-before-create_issue ordering invariant, so future contributors know the contract.
findings.finding: The novel 'dedup-aware callable' pattern (re-running JQL/comment-marker dedup check at the start of every retry) and the changed write protocol (bind_pending written and store persisted BEFORE create_issue) are significant architectural decisions that the plan does not propose capturing in any ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update. Future maintainers changing dispatch_one.py or binding_store.py are at risk of breaking the invariant silently.
findings.location: What § 2 & 3; Scope § (dispatch_one.py, binding_store.py)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or a section in CLAUDE.md / an inline module docstring on _call_with_retry) that states: 'Non-idempotent Jira writes MUST be wrapped in a dedup-aware callable that re-checks the rebar-id JQL / comment-marker before each retry attempt. bind_pending MUST be persisted before create_issue is called.' Reference the audit finding that motivated this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose jitter backoff parameters (max attempts, base delay, Retry-After ceiling) as named constants in a single location (e.g., a config block or `reconciler_config.py`) so they can be tuned without hunting through dispatch_one.py.
findings.finding: The retry backoff parameters (jitter magnitude, maximum retry attempts, Retry-After ceiling) for the HTTPError-aware _call_with_retry extension are neither specified in the ACs nor described as configurable — they will almost certainly be hardcoded constants. Since Jira API rate-limit policy and infrastructure SLAs can change, these are thresholds expected to evolve but the plan provides no path to tune them without a code change.
findings.location: What § 1; AC line 1 ('simulated 500 with jittered backoff')
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small dataclass or module-level constants (e.g., MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS=3, BASE_BACKOFF_S=1.0, RETRY_AFTER_CEILING_S=60) in a shared config module and import them into _call_with_retry — one-line change that makes future tuning self-documenting.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement for the steal() success path: specify that a WARNING-level log entry (including expiry timestamp, previous holder identity if available, and current reconciler identity) is emitted and a metric/counter incremented whenever a lease is successfully stolen.
findings.finding: A successful lease steal is a high-signal operational event (indicates a previous reconciler was SIGKILL'd) but the plan specifies no logging or alerting for it, leaving operators with no visibility into how often crashes occur or which host holds the new lease.
findings.location: What §4 / AC: 'A pass encountering an expired lease steals it via steal() and proceeds'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `__main__.py` line reference for the held-lock exit: the `return 3` path is at lines 499-505 (inside `if advisory.check_pass_lock(repo_root):`), not lines 534-539 (which are variable initialization for the lock acquisition block).
findings.finding: The plan asserts the held-ref lock path is at `__main__.py:534-539` and that it 'unconditionally exits 3', but reading the file shows the `return 3` path is at lines 499-505 (inside `if advisory.check_pass_lock(repo_root):`). Lines 534-539 are variable initialization (`acquired = False`, `lock_oid: str | None = None`, etc.) — not a lock-check exit.
findings.location: Why section, finding #3 ('__main__.py:534-539')
findings.suggested_fix: Update `__main__.py:534-539` → `__main__.py:499-505` in the Why section finding #3 and any AC text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `reconcile.py` line reference for binding persistence: `binding_store.save()` is at line 954, not line 1068 (which is `return result`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'bindings persist only at end of pass (`reconcile.py:1068`)' but `binding_store.save()` is at `reconcile.py:954`. Line 1068 is `return result` — the final return of a result-building helper, not a persistence call.
findings.location: Why section, finding #1 ('bindings persist only at end of pass (reconcile.py:1068)')
findings.suggested_fix: Update `reconcile.py:1068` → `reconcile.py:954` in the Why section finding #1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `run_differs.py` line reference: `recover_pending_bindings(applier)` is at line 351, not line 267; update Why finding #2 and Scope section accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `run_differs.py:267` passes the applier module to `recover_pending_bindings`, but the actual call is at `run_differs.py:351`. Line 267 is inside `_run_differs_invariant_dispatch` — unrelated mutation-processing logic.
findings.location: Why section, finding #2 ('run_differs.py:267 passes the applier module')
findings.suggested_fix: Update `run_differs.py:267` → `run_differs.py:351` in the Why section finding #2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `_call_with_retry` characterization in Why finding #4: it currently retries both `JiraAPIError` AND `TimeoutError` (dispatch_one.py:127-133); the actual gap is that it does not handle `urllib.error.HTTPError`, not that it 'only retries JiraAPIError'.
findings.finding: The plan states `_call_with_retry` 'only retries `JiraAPIError`', but reading `dispatch_one.py:100-144` shows it ALSO catches and retries `TimeoutError` (line 132-133). The claim that 'nothing in the client layer raises [JiraAPIError]' is correct, but the framing that the retry is *only* on JiraAPIError is factually inaccurate and may cause implementers to overlook or double-add TimeoutError handling.
findings.location: Why section, finding #4 ('the applier's `_call_with_retry` only retries `JiraAPIError`, which nothing in the client layer raises')
findings.suggested_fix: Update the phrase to: 'the applier's `_call_with_retry` retries `JiraAPIError` and `TimeoutError` but NOT `urllib.error.HTTPError`, which is what the REST path raises on 429/5xx — so HTTPError transients are never retried on the wrapped write path'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `__main__.py` line reference for the held-lock exit-3 path from `534-539` to `499-505` (the `if advisory.check_pass_lock(repo_root): return 3` block) so the implementer places `steal()` in the right location.
findings.finding: `__main__.py:534-539` is cited as the 'held-ref path that unconditionally exits 3', but the actual exit-3 for the lock-held case is at lines 500–505. Lines 534–539 contain variable initializations (`acquired = False`, `lock_oid: str | None = None`, etc.) inside the `try:` block that runs AFTER the lock-held check has already passed.
findings.location: Why section, Finding #3: 'the held-ref path unconditionally exits 3 (`__main__.py:534-539`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change '`__main__.py:534-539`' to '`__main__.py:499-505`' in both the Why section and the What section item 4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the `run_differs.py` line references from `267` / `268-274` to `351` / `352-357` (the actual `recover_pending_bindings(applier)` call and its surrounding try/except) in the Why section.
findings.finding: `run_differs.py:267` is cited as the line that 'passes the applier module as the client to `recover_pending_bindings`', but the actual `recover_pending_bindings` call is at `run_differs.py:351`. Line 267 is inside a mutation-loop conditional (`if action_str != mut_mod_for_action.MutationAction.repair_property.value:`) — entirely unrelated to binding recovery.
findings.location: Why section, Finding #2: 'run_differs.py:267 passes the applier module (which has no `search_issues`) as the client to `recover_pending_bindings`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'run_differs.py:267 passes the applier module … recover_pending_bindings, so real recovery would AttributeError into a fail-open swallow (run_differs.py:268-274)' to reference lines 351 and 352-357.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the 'acli_rest.py raw-HTTPError raise is unchanged' constraint to its own AC bullet (e.g., 'A regression test asserts that calling acli_rest's HTTP helper with a 429 response still raises raw `urllib.error.HTTPError`, not `JiraAPIError`') so it is independently verifiable and cannot be missed during implementation review.
findings.finding: What §1 devotes three sentences to the instruction 'Do NOT translate HTTPError→JiraAPIError at acli_rest.py' — a cross-file prohibition the implementer must hold in working memory while editing dispatch_one.py. This pink-elephant antipattern creates an instruction-locality gap: the negative constraint is stated in the What section but has no co-located guard or assertion in the AC for acli_rest.py's behavior. The AC1 assertion ('a test asserts the raw HTTPError raise from acli_rest is unchanged') is the right mechanism, but it is buried at the end of a long AC sentence and not surfaced as its own testable line.
findings.location: What §1 ('Do NOT translate HTTPError→JiraAPIError at acli_rest.py') and AC1 (end of first bullet)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
